comment_content:
I hated Teyana Taylor’s part, it was giving shallow and cliché. But Taraji… Taraji didn’t act. She bled.

I found myself weeping like a child, bowling my eyes out. The tension, her wounds wrapped in her breath. There’s something about the way she looks at you, not with her eyes, but with her scars and pain. You see her, not as a character, but as a mirror: every betrayal, every compromise, every desperate silence you’ve buried deep.

In her pain she sought freedom but she lost it completely, together with her bundle of happiness and pain. 

Indeed when it rains it pours,because life doesn’t wait for you to heal before it sends another blow. Misfortune has a haunting sense of timing—it finds you barefoot, tired, and one breath away from breaking.

Two jobs. Ten years. And still nothing.
What kind of world demands so much of a person and gives her so little?

And then I looked at myself.
I remembered my mother.

While Straw is fiction, Taraji’s truth is not. Her truth was a sermon. Her brokenness preached louder than any pulpit. And we must listen.

Her end must not just break our hearts. It must break our indifference,  this film remind us:Kindness is not charity. It is repayment—for the private wars many are fighting.
